summary: Matched the 412-byte Kirby Yoshi neutral-special item file by replacing asm/melee/it/items/itkirbyyoshispecialn.s with src/melee/it/items/itkirbyyoshispecialn.c and adding a public header. The matched C covers it_802F2D98, it_802F2E7C, trivial item-state callbacks it_802F2E68/it_802F2E70/it_802F2E74, and the ItemStateTable it_803F9490. Supporting header/type fixes made ftKb_SpecialNYs_80109238 return Fighter_Part and made it_80274F48 accept Fighter_Part, which let the item attach to the correct Kirby/Yoshi-related fighter part without placeholder typing. The PR also included small formatting/style cleanups in unrelated files, mostly spacing around labels and operators.
what_changed: Added src/melee/it/items/itkirbyyoshispecialn.c with matched C for it_802F2D98, it_802F2E7C, static callbacks it_802F2E68, it_802F2E70, it_802F2E74, and ItemStateTable it_803F9490.
Added src/melee/it/items/itkirbyyoshispecialn.h declaring it_802F2D98, it_802F2E7C, and extern ItemStateTable it_803F9490.
Updated obj_files.mk to build src/melee/it/items/itkirbyyoshispecialn.c.o instead of asm/melee/it/items/itkirbyyoshispecialn.s.o.
Changed ftKb_SpecialNYs_80109238 in ftKb_Init.h from returning void to returning Fighter_Part, matching its use as the fourth argument to it_80274F48 and as input to ftLib_80086630.
Changed it_80274F48 declaration in it_2725.h so its final argument is Fighter_Part instead of int.
Cleaned up adjacent Yoshi item code in it_2F2B.c: compacted the ItemStateTable initializer and changed item->xDD0_flag.bits.b1 assignment from 0 to false.
Removed an unneeded placeholder.h include from it_2F2B.h.
Applied minor formatting fixes in unrelated source files: spacing around operators, macro arithmetic, and generated-style labels such as block_9:, block_13:, block_26:, block_39:, and block_stuff:.
smart_moves: Used the near-identical Yoshi item implementation it_802F2BFC as a template for it_802F2D98; the new file explicitly documents this with a todo noting shared code with it_802F2BFC.
Promoted ftKb_SpecialNYs_80109238 to a typed Fighter_Part return instead of treating the value as a raw int or leaving it as void, making call sites in the matched item code semantically clearer.
Kept the item-state callbacks static inside itkirbyyoshispecialn.c while exposing only the needed entry points and ItemStateTable through the header.
Represented item flag and destroy-type logic with bool/NULL/false-style C where the assembly used raw zero writes, improving readability without changing behavior.

Matched the destructor/removal path by preserving the order of detach/update calls: it_80275070, lb_8000B1CC through ftLib_80086630, it_8027B4A4, it_8027B378, optional destroy_type reset, victim pointer clearing, it_8027CE44, and Item_8026A8EC.
decomp_lessons: When an item file mirrors another character variant, compare the neighboring decompiled item source before hand-translating every asm instruction; here Kirby-Yoshi special-N appears to share structure with it_802F2BFC.

If a function's return value is consumed as a domain-specific enum-like value, update the prototype to the domain type. ftKb_SpecialNYs_80109238 returning Fighter_Part allowed cleaner item attach and ftLib calls.
ItemStateTable entries with no-op callbacks can be expressed as small static bool/void functions returning false or doing nothing, matching short blr/li r3,0 assembly stubs.
Replacing asm with C requires the build-list swap in obj_files.mk; otherwise the matched source will not participate in the text object list.
Removing placeholder includes is a useful follow-up once forward headers and concrete types are sufficient.
For item ownership/attachment code, preserve the distinction between atk_victim and grab_victim fields and clear both during destruction.
naming_conventions: Kept address-based function names: it_802F2D98, it_802F2E68, it_802F2E70, it_802F2E74, it_802F2E7C.
New item source and header use the item-specific path/name itkirbyyoshispecialn.c and itkirbyyoshispecialn.h.
Header guard follows the project address-style pattern: GALE01_2F2D98.
Item state table retains address-based global name it_803F9490.
Typed owner argument for it_802F2D98 as ftKb_GObj* owner_gobj, reflecting the Kirby-specific owner.
Used has_destroy_type as the bool parameter name for it_802F2E7C, matching the conditional reset of item->destroy_type.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: The assembly file's .data table it_803F9490 was converted into a C ItemStateTable array with one entry: { -1, it_802F2E68, it_802F2E70, it_802F2E74 }.
The .sdata2 zero constant it_804DDA10 used for it_8027B378 was represented directly as literal 0 in the C call.
The r31 item data loaded from gobj->0x2C was modeled with Item* item = GET_ITEM(gobj).
Raw writes to item offsets 0xD04 and 0xD00 became item->atk_victim and item->grab_victim assignments.
The bit clear at xDD0 became item->xDD0_flag.bits.b1 = false.
The nested model descriptor loads at xC4->x10->x8 became item->xC4_article_data->x10_modelDesc->x8_bone_attach_id.
The conditional assembly block that zeroed offset 0xD60 when r4 was nonzero became if (has_destroy_type) { item->destroy_type = 0; }.
The final zero writes to victim fields became item->atk_victim = NULL and item->grab_victim = NULL.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR slice.
The PR body reports a 100.00% match for the file: 412 bytes, score 0/10300.
Function-level report in the PR body lists 100.00% for it_802F2D98 at 208 bytes, it_802F2E7C at 184 bytes, it_802F2E68 at 8 bytes, it_802F2E74 at 8 bytes, and it_802F2E70 at 4 bytes.
follow_up_queries: Find other item files with TODOs or comments indicating shared code with it_802F2BFC or it_802F2D98.
Audit other callers of ftKb_SpecialNYs_80109238 to confirm Fighter_Part typing is consistently used.
Search for it_80274F48 call sites still passing raw int values where a Fighter_Part would be clearer.
Look for remaining placeholder.h includes in item headers that can be removed after forward/type cleanup.
Compare Kirby copied-special item implementations for repeated owner attachment/destruction patterns.
